name: MacTutor
biography:
Paul L횄짤vy was born into a family containing several mathematicians. He was the son of Lucien L횄짤vy (1853 - 1912) and Alice Flora Wolff (1856 - 1921) . Lucien and Alice were married on 3 June 1878 . Lucien L횄짤vy had studied at the 횄혟cole Polytechnique from 1872 to 1877 and then had a career as a professor of mathematics, first at the lyc횄짤e of Rennes and then in Paris, at the lyc횄짤e Louis le Grand. As well as teaching in lyc횄짤es, during most of his career he was an examiner at the 횄혟cole Polytechnique. He wrote several books: Arithm횄짤tique횂혻횄혻 l'usage de l'enseignement secondaire sp횄짤cial 창혫혟 ( Arithmetic for use in special secondary education ) 횂 (1880) , 횄혟l횄짤ments d'arithm횄짤tique 창혫혟 ( Elements of arithmetic ) (1883) , Pr횄짤cis 횄짤l횄짤mentaire de la th횄짤orie des fonctions elliptiques avec tables num횄짤riques et applications 창혫혟 ( Elementary precision of the theory of elliptic functions with numerical tables and applications ) (1898) , ( with Eug횄짢ne Rouch횄짤 ) Analyse infinit횄짤simale 횄혻 l'usage des ing횄짤nieurs 횂혻Vol 1 창혫혟 ( Infinitesimal Analysis for Engineers Vol 1 ) (1900) , ( with Eug횄짢ne Rouch횄짤 ) Analyse infinit횄짤simale 횄혻 l'usage des ing횄짤nieurs Vol 2 창혫혟 ( Infinitesimal Analysis for Engineers Vol 2 ) (1902) . He was president of the French Mathematical Society in 1911 . Lucien L횄짤vy was the son of Edouard L횄짤vy (1822 - 1900) and Hortense Breinl횄짤 Cahen (1832 - 1909) . Edouard L횄짤vy, Paul's paternal grandfather, had graduated from the 횄혟cole Normale Sup횄짤rieure in 1843 and also had a career as a professor of mathematics. Paul's mother, Alice Flora Wolff, was the daughter of Samuel Wolff (1827 - 1913) , Inspector General of the Ponts et Chauss횄짤es, and Fanny Goudchaux (1835 - 1888) .
Paul attended three lyc횄짤es in Paris, the lyc횄짤e Montaigne from 1895 to 1898 , the lyc횄짤e Louis le Grand from 1898 to 1902 and the lyc횄짤e Saint Louis from 1902 to 1904 . At the lyc횄짤e Montaigne, L횄짤vy was taught literature and arts subjects and was certainly not the best student in his class, having three brilliant fellow-students, Jacques Massigli, Pierre Lachi횄짢ze and Marc Bloch, who took the top three places. In early 1898 his teacher M de la Filolie told him he had won the Greek prize in the Concours g횄짤n횄짤ral. L횄짤vy wrote [ 2 ] :- It was to be my only literary success. 횄혟mile M횄짖le was the professor of rhetoric at the lyc횄짤e Louis le Grand when L횄짤vy began studying there later in 1898 . His achievement in winning the Greek prize, however [ 2 ] :- ... was not enough to draw the attention of 횄혟mile M횄짖le to me, who, from a great height, let fall on the best in the class a small part of his knowledge of Latin and of archaeological science. It was mathematics that fired L횄짤vy's passion at the lyc횄짤e Louis le Grand. He wrote [ 2 ] :- It is especially in plane geometry that I excelled. I visualise poorly in space, and I do not have the memory to have been impassioned by algebra, which did not prevent me ... to be also, in this discipline, to be at the top of my class. I was passionate about plane geometry; certain theorems were revelations for me: the notion of the power of a point in relation to a circle, that of a radical axis, that of the transformation of a figure by inversion, delighted me. What admirable methods for finding new theorems! I always found the problems easy that were presented to me. I was hoping to have the mathematics prize at the Concours G횄짤n횄짤ral en rhetorique; I did have it, and have not had the opportunity to participate in this competition since. Yet I make a huge difference between what I was when I was in literary studies and what I was going to be the following year. To prepare for entry to the Grands 횄혟coles, L횄짤vy's father moved him from the lyc횄짤e Louis le Grand to the lyc횄짤e Saint Louis for his two final years of schooling. He explained [ 2 ] :- On leaving rhetoric, after having learned the elements of trigonometry during the summer, I entered the Lyc횄짤e Saint-Louis, in a class of elementary mathematics called higher .... My father thought that to prepare for the 횄혟cole Polytechnique, and possibly 횄혟cole Normale Sup횄짤rieure, one should not waste time. I could prepare well for the baccalaureate in philosophy while doing mathematics in the lyc횄짤e. In addition, as far as mathematics was concerned, I would have wasted my time if I had not skipped the preparatory class for the baccalaureate. But the upper class was of a higher standard in Louis Le Grand than in Saint Louis, and, in order not to require excessive effort from me, my father put me in Saint Louis. I have sometimes wondered if it would not have been better if I had stayed at Louis Le Grand. I'm sure I would have easily taken the mathematics class. In his final year at the lyc횄짤e Saint Louis, 1903 - 04 , L횄짤vy was taught by 횄혟mile Blutel (1862 - 1932) . Blutel was a remarkable teacher who went on to publish a number of outstanding textbooks: Le횄짠ons de Math횄짤matiques sp횄짤ciales 횄혻 l'횄혟cole Normale Sup횄짤rieure et des 횄혟tudiants des Facult횄짤s des Sciences 창혫혟 ( Special Mathematics Lessons at the 횄혟cole Normale Sup횄짤rieure and Students of the Faculties of Sciences ) (1914) ; Le횄짠ons de math횄짤matiques sp횄짤ciales 횄혻 l'usage des candidats 횄혻 l'횄혟cole Polytechnique: G횄짤om횄짤trie analytique, courbes et surfaces 창혫혟 ( Special mathematics lessons for the use of candidates to the 횄혟cole polytechnique: Analytical geometry, curves and surfaces ) (1914) ; and Le횄짠ons de math횄짤matiques sp횄짤ciales, candidats 횄혻 l'횄혟cole Polytechnique et 횄혻 l'횄혟cole Normale Sup횄짤rieure: Alg횄짤bre, ligne droite et plan, trigonom횄짤trie, analyse, applications g횄짤om횄짤triques 창혫혟 ( Special mathematics lessons, candidates at the 횄혟cole polytechnique and at the 횄혟cole normale sup횄짤rieure: Algebra, straight line and plane, trigonometry, analysis, geometric applications ) (1914) .
L횄짤vy was placed first for entry to the 횄혟cole Normale Sup횄짤rieur and second for entry to the 횄혟cole Polytechnique in the Concours d'entr횄짤e for the two institutions. He chose to attend the 횄혟cole Polytechnique, considered less prestigious at that time, probably because of his father. While still an undergraduate there, he published his first paper Sur les s횄짤ries semi-convergentes 창혫혟 ( On semi-convergent series ) in 1905 and his second Sur la densit횄짤 des nombres premiers inf횄짤rieurs 횄혻 une grandeur donn횄짤e 창혫혟 ( On the density of prime numbers less than a given number ) in the following year. Writing about his professors, he singled out Georges Humbert [ 2 ] :- The most remarkable of our professors was certainly Georges Humbert , and the course of analysis he gave us was the one from which I benefited the most. His presentations were always remarkably clear. My fellow students even claimed that it was too clear, and that he avoided difficulties that we noticed when studying the course on the sheets that were distributed to us. I only saw once, about the fundamental Jacobian theorem, that this was true. Among our other professors, I will name only Henri Poincar횄짤 and Henri Becquerel. The first was, in theory, a professor of astronomy; but the course was given by a substitute, except for the beginning. Poincar횄짤 himself taught a lesson on spherical trigonometry and two lessons on error theory. After graduating in first place, L횄짤vy took a year doing military service before studying for an engineering degree at the 횄혟cole des Mines in 1907 . While he studied at the 횄혟cole des Mines he also attended courses at the Sorbonne given by Gaston Darboux and 횄혟mile Picard . In addition he attended lectures at the Coll횄짢ge de France by Georges Humbert and Jacques Hadamard . L횄짤vy wrote [ 31 ] :- At the 횄혟cole des Mines, I neglected many of my courses in order to attend mathematics courses at the Sorbonne and at the College of France. I attended Humbert' s course for three years at the College of France. He is known for having been a marvellous calculator, and he contributed to the progress of classical mathematics. I was especially inclined towards the study of modern mathematics, and it was not Humbert' s course that I profited from the most. It was also not Borel 's, which I gave up on after 4 or 5 lectures, because he spent too much time on what was obvious. On the other hand, I profited a great deal from Emile Picard 's course, which I attended for one year, and from Hadamard 's, which I attended for three years. It was in Hadamard 's course, in 1910 , that I found my thesis topic. As he explains, it was Hadamard who was the major influence in determining the topics on which L횄짤vy would undertake research. Finishing his studies at the 횄혟cole des Mines in 1910 he began research in functional analysis . His thesis on this topic, Sur les equations int횄짤gro-differentielles d횄짤finissant des fonctions de lignes 창혫혟 ( On integro-differential equations defining line functions ) , was examined by 횄혟mile Picard , Henri Poincar횄짤 and Jacques Hadamard in 1911 and he received his Docteur 횄짢s Sciences in 1912 .
In 1913 L횄짤vy married Suzanne L횄짤vy (1892 - 1973) , a daughter of the merchant Paul L횄짤vy (1853 - 1903) and Berthe Weil (1862 - 1930) . [ Suzanne really did have a father and a husband with the same name! ] Suzanne's maternal grandfather was Henri Weil (1817 - 1913) , a philologist and member of the Institut de France. Paul and Suzanne L횄짤vy had three children, Marie-H횄짤l횄짢ne ( born November 1913) , Denise ( born 3 October 1916) and Jean Claude ( born 10 April 1918) . Marie-H횄짤l횄짢ne L횄짤vy became a mathematician; she married Laurent Schwartz , taking the name Marie-H횄짤l횄짢ne Schwartz , and has a biography in this Archive. Denise became a professor of German at the lyc횄짤e Moli횄짢re in Paris. She married the engineer Robert Piron ( mentioned below ) . Jean Claude studied at the 횄혟cole Polytechnique and became an engineer in the navy.
L횄짤vy became professor of mathematics at the 횄혟cole des Mines in Paris and a tutor at the 횄혟cole Polytechnique in Paris both in 1913 [ 4 ] :- A tutor's task was to help the students to understand the professors' lectures, and hence L횄짤vy was well acquainted with the curriculum and the methods of instruction at the Polytechnique before his own appointment as professor. World War I caused major disruption in France as it did in most of Europe. L횄짤vy undertook military service and was asked to use his mathematical skills in the war effort. He was assigned practical problems of defence, especially those arising from the then new technology associated with aviation, in particular solving problems concerning defence against attacks from the air. George Humbert , who had taught at the 횄혟cole Polytechnique from 1895 , was one of the professors when L횄짤vy was a tutor but he became seriously ill and he asked L횄짤vy to substitute for him and take over part of his teaching commitments for session 1918 - 19 . Humbert 's poor health led him to resign and L횄짤vy became a full professor of analysis at the 횄혟cole Polytechnique in 1920 . L횄짤vy remained at the 횄혟cole Polytechnique, with certain interruptions described below, until he retired in 1959 .
A young mathematician Ren횄짤 Gateaux was killed near the beginning of World War I and Hadamard asked L횄짤vy to prepare Gateaux 's work for publication. He did this but he did not stop at writing up Gateaux 's results, rather he took Gateaux 's ideas and developed them further publishing the material after the war had ended in 1919 as Publication des oeuvres posthumes de R Gateaux et notes personnelles compl횄짤tant les r횄짤sultats obtenus par cet auteur 창혫혟 ( Publication of posthumous works by R Gateaux and personal notes supplementing the results obtained by this author ) .
As we indicated above L횄짤vy first worked on functional analysis [ 33 ] :- ... done in the spirit of Volterra . This involved extending the calculus of functions of a real variable to spaces where the points are curves, surfaces, sequences or functions. In 1919 , as part of his teaching duties, L횄짤vy was asked to give three lectures at the 횄혟cole Polytechnique on ( see [ 18 ] ) :- ... notions of calculus of probabilities and the role of Gaussian law in the theory of errors. Taylor writes in [ 32 ] :- At that time there was no mathematical theory of probability - only a collection of small computational problems. Now it is a fully-fledged branch of mathematics using techniques from all branches of modern analysis and making its own contribution of ideas, problems, results and useful machinery to be applied elsewhere. If there is one person who has influenced the establishment and growth of probability theory more than any other, that person must be Paul L횄짤vy. The contents of L횄짤vy's lectures are described in [ 4 ] , which the authors end with the following summary:- L횄짤vy's lecture notes at the 횄혟cole Polytchnique in 1919 nicely document his beginnings as a probabilist. Though they are evidently not developed in the detail needed ( we must not forget that L횄짤vy took the charge of the teaching at the drop of a hat ) , they indicate how L횄짤vy perceived probability on the eve of his exceptional second mathematical career. They contain the seeds of the studies L횄짤vy would pursue with virtuosity for 40 years: convergence in distribution, sums of random variables, the connection between mathematical probability and physics. They testify to what L횄짤vy said about himself 50 years later in an interview to the French radio France Culture: "I have the feeling of being a mathematician, neither superior nor inferior to others, but different from them." Lo횄짢ve, in [ 19 ] , gives a very colourful description of L횄짤vy's contributions:- Paul L횄짤vy was a painter in the probabilistic world. Like the very great painting geniuses, his palette was his own and his paintings transmuted forever our vision of reality. ... His three main, somewhat overlapping, periods were: the limit laws period, the great period of additive processes and of martingales painted in pathtime colours, and the Brownian pathfinder period. Not only did L횄짤vy contribute to probability and functional analysis but he also worked on partial differential equations and series. In 1926 he extended Laplace transforms to broader function classes. He undertook a large-scale work on generalised differential equations in functional derivatives. He also studied geometry.
World War II presented serious difficulties for L횄짤vy. On횂 10 횂혻May횂 1940 , Germany invaded Holland, Belgium and Luxemburg then quickly moved to attack France by early June. On횂 14 횂혻June German troops entered Paris and, on횂 22 횂혻June, French Marshal P횄짤tain signed an armistice. This divided France into Vichy France, controlled by a government collaborating with Germany, and a part under direct German control. The 횄혟cole Polytechnique had a certain military status and Germany refused to allow it to remain in Paris. It was relocated to Lyon, in Vichy France, and L횄짤vy moved to Lyon and began teaching. Up to this point we have not mentioned that L횄짤vy was Jewish since it was not relevant. Now, of course, it became a life-threatening difficulty. Legislation of 3 October 1940 required all Jews to be dismissed from their teaching and, on 19 December 1940 L횄짤vy received notice that he could no longer perform his duties. There were exemptions in the 3 October 1940 Act to allow those giving outstanding service to be exempted. An appeal by the director of the 횄혟cole Polytechnique succeeded and from 14 March 1941 he was allowed again to teach.
The situation became worse in the summer of 1942 and L횄짤vy, anticipating events, left Lyon and went to live with his son-in-law Robert Piron, in Montbonnot, near Grenoble in the Italian controlled region of France. He had left Lyon by 4 November ahead of the German invasion of Vichy France on 11 November. He now lived in hiding, carrying false documents, and somehow survived. A letter he wrote to Maurice Fr횄짤chet on 29 November 1943 shows his extreme difficulties [ 25 ] :- I just learned that I'm not a professor at the 횄혟cole Polytechnique anymore. In early November, a postal check issued for my October salary was returned to me as "not sufficient funds". I wrote to the administration of the 횄혟cole to ask for an explanation. On 24 November, I received a copy of a decision dated 30 June by which the 횄혟cole transferred me to the Corps des Mines on 1 October; it is also apparent from the text of the decision that on 29 April the Director of the 횄혟cole knew already that I was not to be "re-invested in my job" and asked the Minister how long he had to pay me!!! And all this is legal, if one considers as legal a 1941 regulation subjecting teachers to a decennial re-election, regardless of acquired rights. And now what will the Corps des Mines do with me, having left the place in 1913 , and cannot give me work for which I have no longer any expertise and, in addition, with a delay of two months before taking up the post. Will it pay me? ... I deeply hope that 1944 will see the end of these miseries. Already by the time L횄짤vy wrote this letter, part of the 횄혟cole Polytechnique was operating again in Paris ( it had returned in April 1943) , but some teaching was still taking place in Lyon for escaped prisoners, Jewish students and others who could not, at that time, return to Paris. After the Allied invasion of France in June 1944 the German occupiers were driven back, and by August of that year the Vichy regime had fallen.
The war years were ones of great difficulties for L횄짤vy, in Lyon, in Montbonnot, near Grenoble, and finally in the Loire, but he was still able to do mathematics [ 25 ] :- In 1940 , L횄짤vy had become one of the world major specialists of stochastic processes. And then suddenly, the 55 years old mathematician who had produced a torrential flow of publications for more than 20 years was deprived of the possibility to send articles to journals. Moreover, as we have seen, his teaching tasks were also gradually reduced, and for some months in 1943 , living in precarious silence in the Italian zone near Grenoble, Paul L횄짤vy had nothing to do other than mathematics while waiting for better days. His luck was to remain in contact with Fr횄짤chet who played for him the role of a private registration journal and could also sometimes serve as a hub between L횄짤vy and other mathematicians. Back in Paris, he continued to teach at the 횄혟cole des Mines until 1951 and at the 횄혟cole Polytechnique until 1959 . There were, however interruptions. He contracted tuberculosis and half his left lung was removed in 1951 . Again in 1957 - 58 he was ill and could not teach, but recovered.
L횄짤vy wrote ten books, the main ones being: Le횄짠ons d'analyse fonctionnelle 창혫혟 ( Lessons in Functional Analysis ) (1922) , Calcul des probabilit횄짤s 창혫혟 ( Calculation of probabilities ) (1925) , Th횄짤orie de l'addition des variables al횄짤atoires 창혫혟 ( Addition theory of random variables ) (1937 ; 1954) , and Processus stochastiques et mouvement brownien 창혫혟 ( Stochastic processes and Brownian motion ) (1948) . For information about all of L횄짤vy's books, including contents of some of them and also extracts from some reviews, see THIS LINK .
For L횄짤vy's views on the calculus of probabilities see the English version of his talk Les fondements du calcul des probabilit횄짤s 창혫혟 ( Foundations of the Calculus of Probabilities ) delivered to an audience at the 횄혟cole Polytechnique in the early 1950 s, see THIS LINK .
For Levy's views on axiomatic set theory and the continuum hypothesis after the work of Kurt G횄쨋del and Paul Cohen, see THIS LINK .
In 1963 L횄짤vy was elected to honorary membership of the London Mathematical Society . In the following year he was elected to the Acad횄짤mie des Sciences .
Lo횄짢ve sums up his article [ 19 ] in these words:- [ L횄짤vy ] was a very modest man while believing fully in the power of rational thought. ... whenever I pass by the Luxembourg gardens, I still see us there strolling, sitting in the sun on a bench; I still hear him speaking carefully his thoughts. I have known a great man. L횄짤vy died in 1971 at the age of 85 . A memorial meeting in his memory was held in the Henri Poincar횄짤 amphitheatre of the 횄혟cole Polytechnique on Friday 23 March 1973 when various talks were given. They were published in La Jaune et la Rouge 창혫혟 ( The Yellow and the Red ) in November 1973 .
For an English version of Laurent Schwartz 's talk, La pens횄짤e math횄짤matique de P L횄짤vy 창혫혟 ( The mathematical thought of P L횄짤vy ) , see THIS LINK .
For an English version of Paul-Andr횄짤 Meyer 's talk L'originalit횄짤 de P L횄짤vy en math횄짤matiques 창혫혟 ( The originality of P L횄짤vy in mathematics ) , see THIS LINK .
For an English version of Benoit Mandelbrot 's talk Paul L횄짤vy, professeur , and another description of L횄짤vy by Mandelbrot , see THIS LINK .
We end this biography of Paul L횄짤vy by quoting an extract from the talk Paul L횄짤vy , delivered by Jean Ullmo (1906 - 1980) at the meeting on 3 March 1973 [ 35 ] :- Paul L횄짤vy was one of the greatest mathematicians of his time and it is an honour for the Corps des Mines to have facilitated the full realization of his exceptional gifts. As a scholar, he was undoubtedly one of the last examples of absolute individualism: he was a solitary researcher who was only concerned with posing problems that interested him and pursuing their solution through only work of interior reflection. He read very little of the work of others, he did not participate in international congresses, except exceptionally at the end of his life. His working methods, artisanal one might say, had their drawbacks: he often found, without knowing it, already known results; more often still, he discovered important results without giving them the necessary publicity, sometimes because he believed them already known. It cannot be denied that there has been wasted effort, but the counterpart has often been admirable: it is the profound originality of a thought indifferent to fashions and schools which did not hesitate to embark on absolutely new paths because he had no fear of solitude. Thus, after having been one of the main precursors of functional analysis, Paul L횄짤vy was the great creator of the theory of probability. It can be said that most of the essential concepts of this theory derive from him.
His teaching at the 횄혟cole Polytechnique, which was of exceptional duration, left a deep mark on his innumerable students: they saw in it a model of conciseness, a requirement vis-횄혻-vis the reader who must recognize all the difficulties hidden in a laconic text; in short, an admirable piece of intellectual gymnastics. Let us add for the record that this very classic course which was little modified until 1957 did not mean that Paul L횄짤vy was losing interest in modern developments in mathematics since he had the courage and the elegance to undertake, in the last two years of his teaching, a redesign of his course to introduce the most recent language and methods.
Finally, we would like to evoke a personal memory: Paul L횄짤vy spoke to me one day about how he chose the questions on which he wanted to focus his research: "I am asking myself a not too difficult problem," he said to me, "so as not to break my teeth in front of an excess of difficulties, but all the same to have to make a big effort which occupies me and gives me the satisfaction of finding something." We will admire this modesty mixed with legitimate pride. These "not too difficult problems" were generally new avenues open to the human mind.
